days.title: Basic commands: give, tell, come

vocab.exampleEn: Take the book from the table.
Wait for me by the door.

grammar.title: The informal imperative (singular)
grammar.explanation: Bulgarian forms commands to one person you know well. Common short forms are дай (give), кажи (tell), ела (come), вземи (take), чакай (wait), седни (sit). These are single-word commands and are very frequent in daily speech. You often add a short pronoun like ми (to me) or ме (me): Дай ми (give me), Чакай ме (wait for me). Remember Bulgarian has no cases, so these pronouns are just fixed little words, not case endings. Adding моля (please) makes any command politer.

reading.title: На вратата (At the door)
reading.text:
— Ела тук! Седни до мен.
— Чакай, дай ми водата, моля.
— Кажи ми, готов ли си?
reading.translation:
— Come here! Sit next to me.
— Wait, give me the water, please.
— Tell me, are you ready?

days.speakingPrompt: Imagine a close friend just arrived at your home. Give three short informal commands inviting them in and asking for help (e.g. ела, седни, дай ми...). Say them aloud, adding моля where it sounds polite.

days.title: More everyday commands

grammar.title: Action commands and the definite article
grammar.explanation: Today's commands act on an object: отвори вратата (open the door), затвори прозореца (close the window). Notice the article is a SUFFIX on the noun: врата → вратата (feminine -та), прозорец → прозорецът / прозореца (masculine -ът full form, -а short form used after verbs). Bulgarian has no cases, so the noun does not change its ending for being an object — only the article suffix marks 'the'. Adverbs like внимателно (carefully) come after the verb: Слушай внимателно.

reading.title: В стаята (In the room)
reading.text:
— Слушай, помогни ми, моля.
— Добре. Отвори вратата и затвори прозореца.
— Погледни тук, готово е.
reading.translation:
— Listen, help me, please.
— Okay. Open the door and close the window.
— Look here, it's done.

days.speakingPrompt: You are tidying a room with a friend. Tell them to open the door, close the window, look at something and help you — using today's commands and моля. Say each sentence aloud.

days.title: Negative commands: don't!

grammar.title: Saying 'don't': не + command and недей
grammar.explanation: To tell someone NOT to do something, the simplest pattern is не + the present-tense verb: Не пипай (don't touch), Не бързай (don't hurry). The verb is in its normal 'you' present form here. Another very common option is недей plus да + verb: Недей да бързаш (don't hurry). For one strong word, недей alone means 'don't!'. The verb спри (stop) is a positive command on its own. Soften any of these with моля or моля те (please, to a friend).

reading.title: В магазина (In the shop)
reading.text:
— Не пипай това, моля.
— Добре, спри да се притесняваш.
— Не бързай, имаме време.
reading.translation:
— Don't touch that, please.
— Okay, stop worrying.
— Don't hurry, we have time.

days.speakingPrompt: A child is about to touch something fragile and is rushing. Give three negative commands (не пипай, не бързай, спри) and add моля те to stay gentle. Say them aloud.

days.title: Welcoming guests: заповядай

vocab.english: here you go / come in / help yourself (informal)
here you go / come in (polite or plural)

grammar.title: Заповядай / заповядайте — the all-purpose welcome
grammar.explanation: Заповядай is one of the most useful words in Bulgarian. It means roughly 'here you are', 'go ahead', 'help yourself', or 'come in', depending on the situation. Use заповядай to one friend, and заповядайте to someone you address politely or to several people — the -те ending marks the polite/plural 'you'. This -те ending is how almost all imperatives become polite or plural: седни → седнете, опитай → опитайте, влез → влезте.

reading.title: На гости (Visiting)
reading.text:
— Влез, заповядай! Седни тук.
— Благодаря.
— Опитай супата, моля. Заповядай в кухнята.
reading.translation:
— Come in, please! Sit here.
— Thank you.
— Try the soup, please. Come into the kitchen.

days.speakingPrompt: A guest arrives at your home. Welcome them aloud using заповядай, влез, седни and опитай, and offer them soup. Then repeat the same welcome politely to two guests using the -те forms.

days.title: Polite requests: моля and Бихте ли

grammar.title: Polite requests with Бихте ли and Можете ли
grammar.explanation: For a polite request to someone you address formally, use Бихте ли + past participle (Бихте ли отворили...? Would you open...?) or, more simply, Можете ли да + present verb (Можете ли да повторите? Can you repeat?). Both are softer and more formal than a bare command. The polite imperatives end in -те: повторете, помогнете, извинете. Моля can stand alone for 'please' or be the verb 'I ask'. A natural reply is Разбира се (of course).

reading.title: На улицата (On the street)
reading.text:
— Извинете, можете ли да помогнете?
— Разбира се. Какво искате?
— Бихте ли повторили, моля?
reading.translation:
— Excuse me, can you help?
— Of course. What do you want?
— Would you repeat, please?

days.speakingPrompt: You are lost in a Bulgarian city and need help from a stranger. Politely get their attention and make two requests using Извинете, Можете ли да... and Бихте ли..., ending with моля. Say it aloud.

days.title: Putting requests together in daily life

vocab.exampleEn: Bring me a glass of water.
Bring me a glass of water.

grammar.title: Combining commands and polite requests in real settings
grammar.explanation: This day combines the week's tools. In a cafe or shop you can mix a polite frame with a clear request: Бихте ли донесли менюто? (Would you bring the menu?) or Донесете сметката, моля (Bring the bill, please). With a friend use the informal form: Донеси ми чаша вода. Word order is request-word + verb + object, and the noun shows 'the' with its article suffix: сметка → сметката, меню → менюто. Add още (more) before the noun: още вода (more water). No cases are involved — only articles and prepositions.

reading.title: В кафенето (In the cafe)
reading.text:
— Извинете, бихте ли донесли менюто?
— Разбира се, заповядайте.
— Донесете още вода и сметката, моля.
reading.translation:
— Excuse me, would you bring the menu?
— Of course, here you go.
— Bring more water and the bill, please.

days.speakingPrompt: You are at a Bulgarian cafe. Politely ask the waiter for the menu, then for more water and the bill, using Бихте ли..., Донесете... and моля. Then say the same requests informally to a friend with донеси. Speak aloud.

days.title: Review: requests & commands

grammar.title: Week 23 recap: imperatives and polite requests
grammar.explanation: This week you learned to make requests and give commands. Informal singular commands: дай, кажи, ела, вземи, чакай, седни, погледни, слушай, отвори, затвори, помогни, опитай, влез, донеси. Make them polite or plural by adding -те: седнете, помогнете, донесете, заповядайте. Say 'don't' with не + present verb (не пипай) or недей да + verb. Welcome people with заповядай / заповядайте. For formal requests use Бихте ли + past participle (Бихте ли донесли...) or Можете ли да + verb (Можете ли да помогнете). Soften everything with моля, and remember: Bulgarian has NO noun cases — relations come from word order, prepositions (в, на, до, с), and the article suffix (-ът/-а, -та, -то, -те).

examples.english: Come here and sit down, please.

reading.title: Гост в кафенето (A guest at the cafe)
reading.text:
— Влез, заповядай! Седни тук.
— Благодаря. Извинете, бихте ли донесли менюто?
— Разбира се. Опитай супата, моля. И не бързай — имаме време.
reading.translation:
— Come in, please! Sit here.
— Thank you. Excuse me, would you bring the menu?
— Of course. Try the soup, please. And don't hurry — we have time.

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play a full scene: welcome a friend into your home (заповядай, влез, седни), offer them food (опитай), then switch to a polite waiter and make requests (Бихте ли..., Донесете..., моля), and finally tell a child not to rush (не бързай, не пипай). Use at least one informal command, one polite -те command, one Бихте ли request, and one negative command. Speak the whole scene aloud.
